planting.note: Sow seeds in fall for spring germination or scarify and sow in early spring; spring vetchling is slow to establish but forms long-lived clumps once settled. It grows naturally in deciduous woodland understory and performs best in similar conditions in the garden — dappled shade with humus-rich, well-drained soil. Plants emerge very early in spring and complete most of their growth before the tree canopy leafs out.

directions: Sow in humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part to full shade; spring vetchling is a small, woodland perennial pea from European forests — one of the earliest-blooming perennials in the spring garden, producing clusters of pea-shaped flowers in a distinctive color change: buds opening deep crimson-purple, flowers fading to violet-blue as they age, creating a plant with two or three colors simultaneously visible. The whole plant is delicate and compact — just 10–14 inches tall — with attractive, divided, vetch-like foliage and a non-climbing, bushy habit unlike most peas. It grows from a woody crown that becomes long-lived, slowly forming tidy, self-contained clumps in the shade garden without spreading aggressively. As a nitrogen-fixing legume, it improves woodland soil fertility. It goes completely dormant by midsummer and disappears underground — an important consideration when siting it, as the empty space can be filled by later-blooming companions. Perfect for the woodland garden alongside trilliums, hepaticas, and bleeding hearts that bloom at the same season.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in early summer or plant container-grown plants in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Himalayan indigo is drought-tolerant and thrives in lean conditions — avoid rich or wet soils that produce lush but floppy growth. In zone 5–6 it may die back to the ground in hard winters but resprouts vigorously from the base in spring.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Himalayan indigo is a large, graceful, arching shrub or subshrub from the mountain forests and open slopes of the Himalayas, producing an extraordinary profusion of rose-pink to magenta-pink, tiny pea flowers in dense, pendulous racemes from June through September — covering the sweeping, arching branches so densely during the flowering peak that the individual branches are almost obscured by bloom. The elegant, pinnate foliage is soft-gray-green throughout the season, creating an attractive textural complement to the vivid flower clusters. As a legume, it fixes nitrogen and improves poor soils. Himalayan indigo is one of the finest long-blooming shrubs for full sun in temperate gardens — combining drought tolerance, extended bloom, attractive foliage, and a graceful weeping habit in a plant that is still relatively little-known outside specialist gardens. Prune hard to the ground or to 6 inches in late winter to maintain compact, vigorous, floriferous growth.

planting.note: Scarify seeds before sowing in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; white prairie wild indigo develops a deep taproot and is very slow the first 2–3 years as it builds root mass. Do not move established plants — the deep taproot makes transplanting almost impossible after the first year. Plants become very long-lived and increasingly spectacular over decades.

directions: Sow scarified seeds in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; white prairie wild indigo is a tall, bushy native legume of the central prairie and open woodland edges, distinct from the Atlantic white wild indigo (Baptisia alba) in its more upright, taller habit and its preference for drier, leaner prairie soils. It produces tall spikes of pure-white pea flowers in May and June that are striking against the three-parted, blue-green leaves — one of the most architecturally impressive native wildflowers in bloom. The inflated seed pods that follow are dark purple to black and ornamentally striking, rattling in the wind and providing excellent dried flower material. As a deep-rooted nitrogen-fixing legume, it improves poor, lean soils over time and is drought-tolerant once established. An important pollen source for native bumblebees, particularly specialist bees that extract pollen by sonication (buzz-pollination) from the flowers. Once established, plants are extremely long-lived — potentially persisting 50 or more years without disturbance.

planting.note: Sow scarified seeds in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; prairie turnip develops a deep, starchy taproot and requires 2–3 years to reach harvestable size. It absolutely requires excellent drainage and resents transplanting once the root has formed. Native to the dry, lean shortgrass and mixed-grass prairies of the Great Plains.

directions: Sow in very well-drained, lean, prairie-type soil in full sun; prairie turnip (tipsin, breadroot) is one of the most important food plants of the North American Plains tribes — a critical starch staple for the Lakota, Blackfoot, Comanche, and many other Plains peoples, who harvested, dried, and braided the turnip-like starchy roots for winter storage. The roots, which resemble parsnips with a brown outer skin and white starchy interior, were eaten raw, boiled, roasted, or ground into flour. Lewis and Clark documented trading for them with the Mandan people. The plant itself is small and unobtrusive — a low-growing perennial with gray-green, divided leaves and small clusters of lavender-blue pea flowers in late spring. The root can reach 2–4 inches in diameter and 4–8 inches in length on mature plants. As a nitrogen-fixing legume of the dry prairie, it improves lean, poor soils. An important plant for food forest, edible prairie, and cultural heritage gardens and for ecologically-minded gardens on the Great Plains.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in fertile, well-drained, moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; rollinia is damaged below 30°F and should be protected from frost. It grows faster and bears fruit at a younger age than cherimoya — typically fruiting within 3–4 years from seed. It tolerates wet soils better than most Annonaceae and thrives in high humidity.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in full sun in frost-free conditions; rollinia (biribá) is a South American tropical tree in the custard apple family, native to the Amazon basin and considered by fruit connoisseurs one of the finest fruits in the Annonaceae family — potentially superior to cherimoya and sugar apple in flavor. The fruits are large (often 4–6 inches across) and round to heart-shaped with distinctive, pointed tubercles (bumps) on the yellow-green exterior that turn golden-yellow at maturity. The interior is snow-white, sweet, translucent, and juicy — much moister and more refreshing than cherimoya — with a flavor combining banana, pineapple, and caramel with a sweet lemon-custard quality. The texture is extremely delicate, almost like thick cream, and it must be eaten immediately after harvest as it deteriorates within 1–2 days of ripening. Rollinia is faster-growing and more prolific than most Annonaceae and is better adapted to humid tropical conditions. It tolerates wet soils and high humidity better than cherimoya or atemoya. An important and growing orchard crop in tropical South America.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Tropical Fruit
